coredata.dc:title: Theoretical study on the utilization of N, O-functionalized graphene quantum dots in Li-S batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Zou, Yiming
Mao, Lei
Yang, Rong
Dang, Jingshuang
Yan, Yinglin
Yu, Wei
Zhong, Lisheng
Xu, Yunhua
coredata.dc:description: Lithium-sulfur (Li-S) batteries have high theoretical capacity and high energy density, and their practical application is difficult because of the “shuttle effect” induced by lithium polysulfides (LiPSs) and the difficulty of insoluble Li2S decomposition. Functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can anchor LiPSs and suppress the “shuttle effect” because of the various polar functional groups. This work investigates the effect of the size of GQDs and the N/O functional groups on the anchoring performance by first-principles calculations. In addition, the influence on the decomposition of Li2S, the diffusion of Li+, and the Gibbs free energy needed for the sulfur reduction reaction was also studied. The results show that the GQDs can effectively anchor LiPSs when the size is larger than 1.44 nm, and all the N/O-functionalized GQDs can also effectively anchor LiPSs. Among these N/O-functionalized GQDs, GQDs-COOH has the smallest Li+ diffusion energy barrier (0.52 eV) and low Li2S decomposition energy barrier (1.12 eV). It indicates that GQDs-COOH enables the rapid diffusion of Li+, ensures the smooth decomposition of Li2S, and catalyzes the effective conversion of LiPSs. Moreover, this work will promote the application of GQDs in Li-S batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-sulfur batteries
Graphene quantum dots
N/O-functionalized
First-principles calculations
Polysulfide

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key R&D Program of Xianyang Science and Technology Bureau

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Program of Xi’an Science and Technology Bureau (2023JH-GXRC-0097), the Key R&D Program of Xianyang Science and Technology Bureau (2021ZDYF-GY-0029), Shaanxi Province Regional Innovation Capacity Guidance Program Project (2022QFY10-06), Program of Xi’an Science and Technology Bureau (23GXFW0066), The National Natural Science Foundation of China (51974242) and Key Program funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (U20A20235). The DFT calculations were supported by the School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi Normal University, China.

This work was supported by Program of Xi'an Science and Technology Bureau (2023JH-GXRC-0097), the Key R&D Program of Xianyang Science and Technology Bureau (2021ZDYF-GY-0029), Shaanxi Province Regional Innovation Capacity Guidance Program Project (2022QFY10–06), Program of Xi'an Science and Technology Bureau (23GXFW0066), The National Natural Science Foundation of China (51974242) and Key Program funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (U20A20235). The DFT calculations were supported by the School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi Normal University, China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Optimized structure of GQDs. (b) Optimized structures of isolated S8 and Li2Sn (n = 8, 6, 4, 2, 1).
Adsorption energies for Li-S composites at different lithiation stages (S8, Li2S8, Li2S6, Li2S4, Li2S2, Li2S) on different GQDs and electrolytes.

Charge transfers when LiPSs adsorbs on GQDs (isovalue=0.015). (a) GQDs-S8, (b) GQDs-Li2S8, (c) GQDs-Li2S6, (d) GQDs-Li2S4, (e) GQDs-Li2S2, (f) GQDs-Li2S. Here, yellow (green) is the spatial region’s gain (loss) in charge.
Adsorption energies for S8 and LiPSs on N/O functionalized GQDs.
Charge transfers when Li2S4 adsorbs on N/O functionalized GQDs (isovalue=0.01). (a) GQDs-OH, (b) GQDs-COOH, (c) GQDs-CO, (d) GQDs-N-Q, (e) GQDs-N-6, (f) GQDs-N-5.
The diffusion energy barrier of Li+ on the surface of N/O-functionalized GQDs.
Spin electron density of (a) GQDs-OH, (b) GQDs-COOH, (c) GQDs-CO, (d) GQDs-N-Q, (e) GQDs-N-6, (f) GQDs-N-5.
The decomposition energy barrier of Li2S on the surface of N/O-functionalized GQDs.
Gibbs free energy for the SRR on N/O functionalized GQDs.
The computed relative energy barriers (Ea1/Ea2) and the relative migration (D1/D2) of Li+ along GQDs-COOH (Ea1, D1) and other functionalized GQDs (Ea2, D2).

head.ce:title: Theoretical study on the utilization of N, O-functionalized graphene quantum dots in Li-S batteries

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: International Research Center for Composite and Intelligent Manufacturing Technology, Institute of Chemical Power Sources, Xi’an University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-sulfur (Li-S) batteries have high theoretical capacity and high energy density, and their practical application is difficult because of the “shuttle effect” induced by lithium polysulfides (LiPSs) and the difficulty of insoluble Li2S decomposition. Functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can anchor LiPSs and suppress the “shuttle effect” because of the various polar functional groups. This work investigates the effect of the size of GQDs and the N/O functional groups on the anchoring performance by first-principles calculations. In addition, the influence on the decomposition of Li2S, the diffusion of Li+, and the Gibbs free energy needed for the sulfur reduction reaction was also studied. The results show that the GQDs can effectively anchor LiPSs when the size is larger than 1.44 nm, and all the N/O-functionalized GQDs can also effectively anchor LiPSs. Among these N/O-functionalized GQDs, GQDs-COOH has the smallest Li+ diffusion energy barrier (0.52 eV) and low Li2S decomposition energy barrier (1.12 eV). It indicates that GQDs-COOH enables the rapid diffusion of Li+, ensures the smooth decomposition of Li2S, and catalyzes the effective conversion of LiPSs. Moreover, this work will promote the application of GQDs in Li-S batteries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-sulfur (Li-S) batteries have drawn a lot of attention as a potential replacement for lithium-ion batteries since they are relatively inexpensive, ecologically benign, have high specific capacity, and have high energy density [1,2]. However, the development of Li-S batteries for commercial use is hampered by the significant “shuttle effect” caused by lithium polysulfides (LiPSs) [3]. Many solutions, including improved cathode materials [4,5], the development of new-type electrolyte systems [6–8], and functionalized interlayers and separators [9,10], have been proposed in recent years to overcome this difficulty. All the work demonstrates that the polar functional groups and heterotopic doping are efficient electrostatic confinement methods for polar LiPSs [11–13]. In recent years, the application of DFT calculation has provided a novel approach to the selection and design of cathode materials for lithium-sulfur batteries. DFT calculation is a method to study the electronic structure of multielectronic systems. This method enables a more in-depth explanation of the mechanism behind the experimental phenomenon at the microscopic level during the development of electrodes, which makes it play an important role in the research and development process [14,15].

Graphene modified with heterotopic atoms such as N, O, Cl, and P, has been extensively explored as a cathode material for Li-S batteries [16]. N and O-doped graphene materials are widely used in Li-S batteries to mitigate the “shuttle effect” [17,18]. The interactions between B, N, O, F, S, P, and Cl atom-doped graphene nano matrices and Li2S, Li2S4, Li2S8, S8 were investigated by Qiang Zhang et al. to model graphene with different heteroatom doping morphologies [19]. The binding energy, configuration, bond length, and differential charge density between doped graphene and polysulfide were investigated based on the density functional theory (DFT) calculations. The results show that N and O atoms can have strong electrostatic interactions with Li atoms of LiPSs, which can lead to the adsorption of LiPSs, while B, P, S, and Cl atoms have weaker interactions with LiPSs. It explains why N and O doping is predominant in literature reports. To further investigate the interaction between N atoms and LiPSs, Yin et al. demonstrated that the binding energy between isolated N-dopants graphene and LiPSs is weaker than that of clustered pyridinic N-dopants by DFT theoretical calculations [20].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a new type of graphene material with a particle size of less than 10 nm. It has the advantages of both graphene and quantum dots, such as large specific surface area, high electrical conductivity, and easy modification. Therefore, it is widely used in many fields such as bio-imaging, photoelectric sensing, and energy storage [21,22]. In 2016, Park's team introduced GQDs to the sulfur cathode of Li-S batteries for the first time to improve battery electrochemical performance [23]. The addition of GQDs ensures the integrity of the electrode structure. The layered architecture facilitates faster charge transfer and reduces the loss of active substances. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) test results and DFT calculations demonstrate that GQD can also anchor LiPSs by forming C-S bonds with LiPSs. This work shows that GQDs can be used in Li-S battery cathodes to enhance electrochemical performance. Moreover, Hu et al. introduced GQDs into the anode of Li-S batteries and illustrated that the addition of GQDs effectively inhibited the growth of lithium dendrites while improving the cycling stability of the batteries [24]. However, as a new graphene material, the mechanism of GQDs in enhancing the electrochemical performance of Li-S batteries still needs to be studied in depth.

Numerous pieces of literature have been published in recent years to simulate the role of graphene in Li-S batteries using first principles, but there are no theoretical calculations related to the application of GQDs in Li-S batteries reported so far. The particle size of GQDs currently reported in the literature is around 1–5 nm [25–27], which provides an accurate experimental basis for modeling. The properties of quantum dots depend on their size, shape, composition, and structure. Therefore, investigating the effect of the size and functionalization of GQDs on the performance through simulations can promote the application of GQDs in lithium-sulfur battery cathodes. To explore the optimal size of GQDs, this work modeled GQDs with different lateral dimensions and investigated their anchoring ability to LiPSs by charge transfer and differential charge density for a deeper mechanistic analysis. It was found that the size of GQDs affects both physisorption and chemisorption and when the size reaches above 1.44 nm it can effectively adsorb LiPSs and suppress the “shuttle effect”. Based on the explored size of GQDs, the GQDs with O and N functional groups were modeled separately. The roles of O and N functionalized GQDs are elucidated through theoretical studies of adsorption energy, charge transfer, lithium-ion diffusion energy barrier, and decomposition energy barrier of Li2S. The results show that GQDs-COOH enables the rapid migration of Li+ and the smooth decomposition of Li2S, ensuring high utilization of S during the charging and discharging process. It is hoped that this work will provide a theoretical basis for the application of GQDs in Li-S batteries.

All DFT calculations are performed by the all-electron method in the DMol3 code within Materials Studio from BIOVIA Inc [28]. The Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) generalized function in the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) is used to handle exchange-correlation interactions [29,30], the most common and general density generalization in material science. This work uses empirical corrections from the Grimme method to characterize the possible van der Waals interactions between various Li2Sn species and GQDs. The convergence threshold parameters for energy, force, and displacement in structure optimization are 1 × 10−5 Ha, 2 × 10−3 Ha/ Å, and 5 × 10−3 Å. The all-electron double numerical plus polarization group (DNP) is used to describe the wave function of valence electrons with an accuracy comparable to that of the 6–31 G** basis group [31,32], which agrees well with experimental results. The convergence range of the self-consistent field (SCF) is set to 1×10−6 Ha. The quality of the orbital truncation is set to fine to ensure high-quality calculation results. The energy barrier for lithium-ion diffusion and lithium sulfide decomposition on the GQDs is calculated using Linear Synchronous Transit and Quadratic Synchronous Transit (LST/QST) methods. All simulations used GQDs combined with LiPSs, where the GQDs are closed off using hydrogen atoms. The charge population was computed by using the Hirshfirld method [33].

The adsorption energy (Eads) was computed to measure the adsorption strength between the GQDs and the LiPSs [34]. It can be expressed as:
(1)
E
ads
=
E
GQDs + LiPSs
−
E
GQDs
−
E
LiPSs
where E
GQDs+LiPSs , E
GQDs , and E
LiPSs represent the energy of the GQDs-LiPSs adsorbed system, the summation of pure LiPSs and pure GQDs. With this definition, a more negative adsorption energy indicates a more energetically favorable adsorption interaction of the LiPSs with the GQDs.

For the visualization of charge transfer, the charge density difference is calculated according to the following formula:
(2)
Δ ρ =
ρ
AB
–
ρ
A
+
ρ
B

The ρ
AB , ρ
A , and ρ
B in the equation are the changes in spatial distributions of charge density in LiPSs absorbed GQDs, isolated LiPS molecules, and free GQDs.

The Arrhenius equation calculates the Li+ diffusion coefficient [35].
(3)
D ∼ exp
−
E
a
K
B
− 1
T
− 1
Where D is the diffusion constant of Li+ [36], E
a is the diffusion energy barrier, K
B is the Boltzmann constant, and T is 298.15 K.

The Gibbs free energy change (ΔG) of the sulfur reduction reaction (SRR) was calculated by the following formula:
(4)
Δ G = ∆ E + ∆ ZPE − T ∆ S
where ΔE, ΔZPE, and TΔS represent the total energy, zero-point vibrational energy, and entropy changes calculated by DFT, respectively. The temperature is 298.15 K.

To explore the optimal GQD size, graphene quantum dot models of different sizes were constructed. Named according to the number of intermediate six-membered rings, respectively as GQDs-3, GQDs-5, GQDs-7, GQDs-9, and GQDs-11. The constructed GQDs and LiPSs models are shown in Fig. 1. The lateral dimensions of GQDs are 0.96, 1.44, 1.93, 2.42 and 2.91 nm, respectively. Graphene quantum dots maintain a planar configuration and do not undergo any distortion as their size increases. The Li2Sn species studied in this paper are all in three-dimensional clusters rather than chains, which matches the structure calculated by the VASP software and indicates the accuracy of our constructed model [36]. The charge occupancy of these LiPSs clusters was analyzed using the Hirshfeld method. The results show that about 0.42, 0.47, 0.57, 0.71, and 0.74 |e| are transferred from lithium to sulfur atoms as the lithiation increases. This indicates that the ionic character of LiPSs gradually increases during the discharge process.

The adsorption behavior of LiPSs on the surface of GQDs of different sizes was investigated. Multiple adsorption configurations were tested for each LiPSs to obtain the most stable adsorption configuration (as shown in Fig. S1). The adsorption energy corresponding to the most stable adsorption conformation is shown in Fig. 2. The results show that the adsorption energy of GQDs on LiPSs increases with the increase in GQDs' size. It is because the increase in the size of GQDs leads to a growth of van der Waals forces with LiPSs. Since GQDs adsorb S8 mainly by van der Waals forces, the change in adsorption energy is more prominent on S8. However, the adsorption energy of GQDs on soluble long-chain LiPSs gradually decreases with increasing lithiation. This is due to the gradual decrease of sulfur atoms, which reduces the van der Waals interactions. Finally, the adsorption energies of all the GQDs except GQDs-3 for LiPSs were larger than those of DOL and DME, which means that GQDs (5, 7, 9, 11) were able to adsorb LiPSs effectively.

To thoroughly understand the reason for the change in adsorption energy, the charge transfer between GQDs and LiPSs was calculated (as shown in Table S1.). The calculation results show that the charges are transferred from GQDs to LiPSs except for S8. There is almost no charge transfer between GQDs and S8. In addition, as the lithiation of LiPSs proceeds, the amount of charge transfer also seems to show certain patterns. To further understand the effect of charge transfer on the adsorption energy, charge-different density analyses with GQDs-5 were performed as an example. Fig. 3a shows no significant charge transfer between GQDs-5 and S8 was observed, again demonstrating the physical adsorption between GQDs and S8. From the (b-c) plots, it can be found that there is a charge-enriched region between soluble Li2Sn (n= 4, 6, 8) and GQDs, which indicates that GQDs chemisorbed them to some extent. However, charge enrichment zones also exist between insoluble Li2Sn (n= 1, 2) and GQDs, and the S atom loses electrons. (Fig. 3e, 3f). It indicates that the electron-binding ability of the polysulfide ion is positively correlated with the number of S atoms. Table S1 gives the results of the charge transfers when LiPSs adsorbs on GQDs. The results show that the amount of charge transfer decreases with the increase in the size of GQDs. differential charge density analysis was further performed using Li2S4 as an example (Fig. S2). As described in Fig. S2, during the adsorption of Li2S4, the electrons of GQDs-3 were internally transferred and re-enriched between them and the lithium atoms, while the sulfur atoms also captured some of the electrons. A similar situation occurred with GQDs-5. When the larger size GQDs (7, 9, 11) adsorb to Li2S4, the GQDs lose fewer electrons, and instead, the S atoms in Li2S4 start to contribute electrons. This proves that the size of GQDs determines their electron-absorbing capacity.

From the previous discussion, it can be seen that the larger the size of the GQDs, the stronger the electron-absorbing ability, and that all the GQDs except GQDs-3 can effectively anchor LiPSs. Therefore, this work further constructed O-functionalized and N-functionalized GQDs based on GQDs-5 to investigate the performance of O-functionalized and N-functionalized GQDs as anchoring materials for Li-S batteries. This work constructed O-functionalized GQDs containing hydroxyl (GQDs-OH), carbonyl (GQDs-CO), and carboxyl (GQDs-COOH) groups and N-functionalized GQDs containing graphitic-N (GQDs-N-Q), pyridinic-N (GQDs-N-6), and pyrrolic-N (GQDs-N-5), as shown in Fig. S3. To determine whether N/O functionalized graphene quantum dots have an anchoring effect on lithium polysulfide, the anchoring behavior of N/O functionalized GQDs on LiPSs was investigated in detail. The calculated results are shown in Fig. 4. The results indicate that the adsorption energy of N/O-functionalized GQDs on LiPSs is greater than that of DOL and DME, which indicates that all N/O-functionalized GQDs are capable of effective adsorption on LiPSs. In N-functionalized GQDs, GQDs-N-5 and GQDs-N-6 have stronger anchoring properties for LiPSs, while GQDs-N-Q has relatively weaker anchoring ability. Among the O-functionalized GQDs, GQDs-CO and GQDs-COOH have stronger anchoring abilities to LiPSs than GQDs-OH.

The adsorption of Li2S4 on N/O functionalized GQDs is used as an example to understand the adsorption energy. Table S1 lists the distance of different functionalized GQDs from Li2S4. GQDs-N-6 and GQDs-N-5 are strongly adsorbed to Li2S4, so the distances between them and Li2S4 are only 1.69 Å and 1.70 Å. The adsorption energies of GQDs-CO, GQDs-COOH, and GQDs-OH for Li2S4 are slightly weaker and thus present longer distances of 2.10 Å, 2.12 Å, and 2.18 Å, respectively. GQDs-N-Q has the weakest adsorption energy for Li2S4 and therefore has the longest distance of 2.22 Å. From the analysis, it can be seen that the stronger the adsorption energy, the closer the GQDs are to Li2S4.

Differential charge density analysis may be able to explain the strength of adsorption, and the results are shown in Fig. 5. The charge transfer of GQDs-N-5 and GQDs-N-6 is similar, both transferring from S in Li2S4 to GQDs and forming a charge enrichment region between Li and GQDs. The wider range of charge enrichment is responsible for the higher adsorption energy. When O-functionalized GQDs adsorb Li2S4, the charge is mainly enriched in two regions, one between Li atoms and GQDs and the other between Li atoms and GQDs, with most of the electric charge originating from the O-functional group and a small portion from Li2S4. It indicates that O-functional groups are the main source of charge when adsorption occurs. Moreover, the charge-enriched region between GQDs-N-Q and Li2S4 is the smallest and thus exhibits the lowest adsorption energy.

From the above discussion, it can be found that GQDs can effectively adsorb LiPSs by physical and chemical adsorption when their size is above 1.44 nm, which means the number of C atoms in GQDs needs to reach 54 and above. In addition, the N/O-functionalized GQDs are also able to adsorb LiPSs effectively.

The ideal electrode material for Li-S batteries needs high catalytic activity for Li2S decomposition and rapid diffusion of Li+ on its surface to ensure higher utilization of S during the charging and discharging process[37,38]. Therefore, the decomposition energy barrier of Li2S and the diffusion potential barrier of Li-ion were calculated by the LST/QST method. Herein, the diffusion paths of Li+ are set based on structural optimization, and Fig. S4 shows the diffusion paths of Li+ on N/O-functionalized GQDs. The diffusion energy barrier corresponding to the Li+ diffusion path is shown in Fig. 6. The results showed that GQDs-N-5 had the highest Li+ diffusion energy barrier (4.63 eV), which indicates that Li+ is difficult to migrate on the surface of GQDs-N-5, which leads to poor rate performance. GQDs-CO, GQDs-OH, GQDs-N-Q, and GQDs-N-6 had similar Li+ diffusion energy barriers (1.42, 1.35, 1.33, and 1.28 eV, respectively), while GQDs-COOH had the lowest Li+ diffusion energy barrier (0.52 eV) among functionalized GQDs. It indicates that GQDs-COOH can accelerate the ionic conductivity and thus improve the electrochemical reaction kinetics.

For elucidating the kinetic differences of various materials, the Arrhenius equation was used to calculate the Li+ diffusion coefficient. Table 1 compares the GQDs-COOH with other functionalized GQDs.

As shown in Table 1, the diffusion mobility of Li+ in GQDs-COOH is nearly 70 orders of magnitude faster than that of GQDs-N-5 and more than 1012-1015 orders of magnitude faster than that of the other N/O-functionalized GQDs. A lower diffusion energy barrier can lead to a faster diffusion rate, thus improving the poor cycling of the Li-S battery. Moreover, GQDs with different functional groups have different Li adsorption strengths with Li in LiPSs, which in turn affects the diffusion energy barrier. To explain these observations, spin electron density calculations have been performed. For O-functionalized GQDs, the results of spin density have shown the distribution for the unpaired electrons which will impede the diffusion of Li+. As shown in Figs. 7a-7c, this spin density is more localized at the C atom close to the carbonyl group in GQDs-CO. This is similar to the situation of the hydroxyl group in GQDs-OH, but the most localized area is a little further away. However, in GQDs-COOH the situation is quite different, there is almost no spin density near the carboxyl group. Therefore, when the Li+ diffuse follows the arrow direction which is the same diffusion pathway as the energy barrier has been calculated, the Li+ will have the largest resistance in GQDs-CO and the smallest in GQDs-COOH. Moreover, N-functionalized will introduce an extra electron to the GQDs, the distribution of the spin density is more complicated as shown in Figs. 7d-7f. Partial results for the GQDs-N-Q, GQDs-N-6, and GQDs-N-5 are the same for the spin density localized around the N atom. However, there is a strong localization area at the N atom for GQDs-N-5, which will cause a strong resistance to Li+ diffusion. The spin density results are quite coincident with the Li+ diffusion energy barriers in different functionalized GQDs, in which high-level spin density leads to a large energy barrier.

In the charge/discharge cycle of Li-S batteries, the conversion from insoluble short-chain LiPSs to soluble long-chain LiPSs or sulfur monomers as the final product requires a large amount of activation energy[39]. This severely slows down the reaction rate. Li2S2 and Li2S deposited on the surface of the sulfur cathode further increase the internal resistance. Therefore, the decomposition of Li2S is also crucial. To predict the reversibility of N/O-functionalized GQDs in batteries, the decomposition energy barriers of Li2S on the surface of different GQDs and non-functionalized GQDs-5 are investigated to evaluate the kinetic reactions of surface de-lithium.

In the simulation, Li2S is completely decomposed into a LiS cluster and a Li-ion (Li2S→LiS+Li++e-). The decomposition path is shown in Fig. S5. The decomposition energy barrier of Li2S is shown in Fig. 8. It can be seen that the decomposition energy barrier of Li2S on the surface of GQDs-CO and GQDs-COOH is significantly lower than 3.05 eV (GQDs-5). It means that Li2S can be decomposed more rapidly on the surface of GQDs-CO and GQDs-COOH, thus improving the utilization of S in the charge/discharge cycle.

To gain a deeper investigation of the role of different functionalized GQDs on the kinetics of the sulfur reduction reaction during the discharge process, an overall reaction based on the reversible formation of Li2S from S8 and Li bulk was considered. The transformation at each step of the SRR process was simulated. The Gibbs free energy required for the reaction was calculated ( Fig. 9).

In the process of converting S8 to Li2S8, the first step is the discharge reaction. In GQDs-5, the Gibbs free energy change for this reduction step is −3.07 eV. The other four GQDs have ΔG values of −3.11 eV (GQDs-OH), −3.14 eV (GQDs-COOH and GQDs-N-6), and −3.21 eV (GQDs-N-5). This suggests that the reduction reaction of S8 is more energetically favorable in these four GQDs, which speeds up the forward reaction and improves the reaction kinetics. In particular, out of all the GQDs, only GQDs-N-Q display heat absorption (−0.06 eV) when Li2S8 is converted to Li2S6. The point in the SRR process where ΔG reaches its highest value is known as the rate-limiting step (RLS). For GQDs-OH, GQDs-CO, and GQDs-N-Q, RLS is the reduction step from Li2S4 to Li2S2, exhibiting ΔG of 1.36 eV, 1.26 eV, and 1.16 eV, respectively. The RLS of GQDs-COOH, GQDs-N-6, and GQDs-N-5 is a reduction step from Li2S2 to Li2S, they correspond to ΔG of 1.09 eV, 1.39 eV, and 0.96 eV, respectively. Compared to GQDs-5 (1.30 eV), the GQDs-OH and GQDs-N-6 retard the reaction causing a polarization detrimental to redox kinetics. The ΔG values of GQDs-CO, GQDs-COOH, GQDs-N-Q, and GQDs-N-5 are lower than that of GQDs-5, indicating that the above four functionalized GQDs could enhance the redox kinetics of LiPSs. Moreover, among them, GQDs-COOH and GQDs-N-5 have the lowest ΔG values and would catalyze the conversion of LiPSs better.

In summary, a detailed study of the anchoring effect of GQDs of different sizes on LiPSs was first conducted in this paper. Different sizes of GQDs lead to distinct adsorption characteristics of LiPSs, and the adsorption intensity is determined by both physical and chemical adsorption provided by GQDs. LiPSs can be effectively adsorbed when the size is 1.44–2.91 nm. Based on determining the suitable size of GQDs, the anchoring effect, electronic structure, Li+ diffusion, and Li2S decomposition of LiPSs on N/O-functionalized GQDs were thereafter studied. Calculations show that all functionalized GQDs can adsorb LiPSs effectively, with GQDs-N-6 and GQDs-N-5 having the strongest adsorption energy due to the large charge transfer from S atoms to them. The adsorption capacity of oxygen-functionalized GQDs on LiPSs mainly comes from the electron-withdrawing capacity of O-functional groups. Among them, GQDs-COOH is the strongest. In contrast, GQDs-COOH possesses the smallest Li+ diffusion energy barrier and low Li2S decomposition energy barrier. GQDs-CO, GQDs-COOH, GQDs-N-Q, and GQDs-N-5 were all able to catalyze the conversion of LiPSs, with GQDs-COOH and GQDs-N-5 being the most effective. Our work shows that the performance of GQDs varies for different functionalizations. Therefore, when using GQDs in Li-S battery cathode materials, concentrations of nitrogen and oxygen functional groups should be carefully thinking about so that the adsorption and catalytic properties can exert a synergistic effect.

Yiming Zou: Writing – original draft. Lei Mao: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation, Conceptualization. Rong Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Lisheng Zhong: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yunhua Xu: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Jingshuang Dang: Validation, Software. Yinglin Yan: Supervision. Wei Yu: Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Program of Xi’an Science and Technology Bureau (2023JH-GXRC-0097), the Key R&D Program of Xianyang Science and Technology Bureau (2021ZDYF-GY-0029), Shaanxi Province Regional Innovation Capacity Guidance Program Project (2022QFY10–06), Program of Xi’an Science and Technology Bureau (23GXFW0066), The National Natural Science Foundation of China (51974242) and Key Program funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (U20A20235). The DFT calculations were supported by the School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi Normal University, China.
